content: 9. Statement of Then Mayor of Kathmandu Metropolitan City, Balendra Shah 'Balen':

On Bhadra 23 and 24, 2082, I was at the official residence in Lainchaur. I supported the peaceful rally on the 23rd but was not part of the movement. The Metropolitan Police Force distributed water at the request of businesses and restaurants. I learned through media in the evening that the situation was becoming tense on the 23rd. My expressions on social media helped calm the uncontrolled movement. I appealed through social media for restraint for the protection of lives and property. After the incidents, in coordination with the Metropolitan Police Force and other departments, immediate necessary assistance was provided to families of the injured and deceased. The events of the 24th were unfortunate and may be the result of excessive use of force and a terrorist-style approach by the government on the 23rd. On the 24th, KMC had dispatched fire trucks in a timely manner and the metropolitan's own fire trucks controlled structures within the metropolis. At the request of the Honorable President and the Army Chief, I played a role in calming the movement. The then government engaged in criminal and terrorist activities and the then government and participating parties must take responsibility for all the damage. The use of the word 'burn it down' was not used in a Facebook expression I gave as a metaphor in protest after the garbage truck of Kathmandu Metropolitan was unnecessarily stopped on its way to Sisdol.
10. Incident Account of Former Prime Minister Jhala Nath Khanal:

After the People's Movement of 062-063 in our country, the fact that we built a constitution through a Constituent Assembly is the greatest achievement in history  something that has happened in only 40-42 countries in the world so far. After the constitution was made, the first election was held, and approximately two parties obtained a two-thirds majority; the government they formed was unable to work for the transformation of the country and for the proper conduct of international relations. The land reform necessary for the country did not happen. Agriculture in this country was not modernized. Similarly, there was a need to take the country toward industrialization  nothing was done. Even the industries that existed  some of which had been established in Nepal with grants and assistance from socialist China and the formerly socialist Soviet Union as gifts  were sold off for almost nothing and consumed by certain elements, while new industries could not come to the country, resulting in employment failing to develop anywhere in the country and the economy becoming paralyzed, creating a situation where only middlemen held the economy's reins. Corruption spread throughout society, laws and regulations to control it could not be enacted, no improvement was made in education or health, and large numbers of the country's youth were forced to go abroad. After this kind of situation began to emerge, widespread dissatisfaction, protest, and anger among the people was natural. Contradictions were growing and governments were unable to address the discontent by taking it seriously. Exploiting this with great subtlety, some foreign powers worked on how to take control of the country and made preparations over a very long time  in the course of making those preparations, many institutions are involved. In America, a magazine called 'Grey Zone' has been published, which explains this chain of events in Nepal in detail and has leaked a secret report of all the events that occurred in Nepal. What kinds of institutions were behind the destruction of the 24th can also be learned from that. And the events of the 23rd and 24th are a living, vivid example of how foreign powers can exploit internally developing dissatisfaction among the Nepali people. I heard that on the 23rd, the Gen Z brothers and sisters were demonstrating and had placed three demands  end to corruption, establishment of good governance, and the opening of 26 social media platforms.

Watching on TV, they had initially gathered peacefully. But I was surprised to see boys and girls in uniforms also coming to the movement  though they were doing it peacefully. As the peaceful, growing movement proceeded, the moment students reached in front of Everest Hotel, those bearing 'TOB' inscriptions entered there and it became chaotic. After that, at Baneswhar Chowk, people gathered from all sides, and ruffians had taken over the crowd. Then they began advancing toward the Parliament building and entering it, started vandalism, and clashes erupted. After that, water cannons began firing, tear gas was released, and simultaneously gunfire began. Those were possibly rubber bullets  there was chaotic fleeing to dodge the bullets. The procession is in front, the police are also in front, something like clashes are occurring in between. But in the middle of a dense crowd, if someone is hit by a bullet in the middle  you can't see who fired  but after someone falls, they must have fallen because of a bullet. How did bullets fly there? But 6-7 days later, I got to read an interview with a Gen Z brother who said that right in front of him, a bullet hit one brother who fell and was taken away by friends, and then another was fired  that bullet, he says, was fired from a window of a distant tower. In that interview, he says the window was opened and bullets were fired and then the window was closed  which could be important evidence.

Even after hearing that police were burning leaders' houses on the 24th, we did not go anywhere. At around 11:30 AM, while we were sitting on the third floor of our house, a military van came downstairs saying they had come to rescue us. After being told we might be attacked and should leave, I said my son and wife were also there, but they pulled me away saying there was no time. Within 45 minutes, my house and car were burned; my family members went even above the third floor and cried and screamed but there was nobody to care. In the meantime, when I called my son Nirbikal, he said 'Father, they're burning us' and the phone went dead. My wife had lost consciousness. Her windpipe was burned all the way down. For two and a half months she had no voice. Since she had fallen face-down when she lost consciousness, the upper part did not burn. My wife's left hand was burned to the fourth degree; she was not even counted as injured by the so-called current government. Police brothers connected a ladder from the neighboring roof to the third floor of my house and, entering through it, after rescuing my son and one police brother, on the fifth attempt the police below finally found my wife lying there and carried her out. From there, she was placed on a ladder, pulled, and taken to another house. Even when trying to take her to the hospital in a neighbor's car, murderers who wouldn't even let her be saved  shouldn't this government arrest and take action against them? What reason was there to kill my wife? Having seen many difficulties during her treatment, we took her to Apollo for further treatment. She no longer has her hand  the left hand had to be amputated. The right hand, even without amputation, is not in a state to function. For forty years she lectured on physics. During all this time, what corruption, what crime, what did she do for any foreigner, and why was she subjected to this kind of attack?

The events of the 24th are the greatest catastrophe to have occurred in this country. Since 1950, for 104 years, the Ranas looted the entire population and built palaces  and the revolutionaries of 1950 did not throw even a single stone at those palaces. In 1990, a movement occurred  for 30 years the Panchayat rulers who looted the country and governed it as tyrants built palaces  not a single stone was thrown at them. Then in 2006-2007, a major movement occurred, and the Maoist struggle that began in 1996 continued for 10 years. During that time, there was vandalism and so on, but the movement of 2062-63  which we call a revolution  brought three important changes to the country: first, the end of feudalism in the economic sphere, second, the abolition of the monarchy in the political sphere and the establishment of a republic, and third, the making of the Nepali state into a democratic state. This was accomplished by political parties that had worked for a long time among the people for national transformation, and even as those changes came, not a single stone was thrown at anyone. On the 24th, Singha Durbar was destroyed, the Supreme Court was destroyed, important documents were selectively burned. Public institutions were burned, public buildings were burned, over 500 police buildings were burned, over 90,000 bullets were seized from the police themselves, thousands of SLR rifles were seized. And over 10,000 prisoners  among whom were those convicted of serious crimes  were released in broad daylight. They have not yet come into government hands and a large number are still outside. But during this destruction, why and how was it that not a single Royalist house, not a single house of Rastriya Prajatantra Party members, was touched  how saintly they must be. And nothing happened to anyone from the Rastriya Swatantra Party  they too must be very saintly.

And buildings of municipalities in all other areas were burned, but while the house of the Deputy Mayor of Kathmandu Metropolitan City was vandalized, the Mayor's house remained untouched  how fortunate a Mayor! How were specific selected ones protected? What was the reason the statue and library at Champadevi, built in the name of Comrade Pushpalal, had to be burned? Some individual leaders whose houses were also burned  why was that done? Alongside this, the application called Discord  built around 2014-15 for gaming  was first used for political purposes in Ukraine. Then it was used in the so-called 'color revolutions' in Arab countries during the Arab Spring, and then in various third-world countries for regime change. Some time ago it was done in Sri Lanka, then in Bangladesh, and finally in our Nepal. That app used children of specific private schools  not government ones. The Gen Z movement is not something for Nepalis to take pride in. Nepalis were misused and destroyed, but we cannot blame technology  it depends on how it is used.

The criminals must be arrested with evidence and facts, brought before the bar of justice, and given necessary punishment  only then will the Nepali people find peace. Otherwise, the series of such events that has begun in this country is the beginning of a terrible calamity. To stop it, everything must be traced to its roots and countermeasures must be adopted against it. If a comprehensive package cannot be built in every sector, this cannot be countered. Therefore, a team of scholars must be formed and we must build a counter package. The attacks on Sher Bahadur Deuba and Arju Deuba are products of that bad culture. If Sher Bahadur had made some mistakes in the course of his duties, I am not aware of them  but if he had, the law should give the punishment for that, not deliver it in a forcible and inhuman manner. Is the Gen Z movement about delivering punishment in a violent and inhumane way? This is the extreme of inhumanity.
11. Account of Rajya Laxmi Chitrakar (Wife of Former Prime Minister Jhala Nath Khanal):

On Bhadra 24, while stone-pelting was occurring at the house, we did not know who had come  we were sitting in a place where stones could not reach us. They must have come to kill us; I lost consciousness because I could not even scream. The moment the fire touched me, my hand burned immediately and I lost consciousness unable to breathe. It must have taken the police brothers about an hour to find me. According to eyewitnesses, a Nepal Police Sub-Inspector Santosh Rupakheti, Saroj Khadka, and Armed Police Force's Bimal Pant, along with other police personnel, searched for and found me. Even now my body burns and I cannot do my own work. My right hand does not move properly. How could someone, without the involvement of a person who knows where important documents and treaties are kept in a place like Singha Durbar, without knowing what is on a computer, smash that very computer, take the hard disk, and commit arson? How can the government hold elections and decide who wins while keeping criminals free? The government must be vigilant in bringing criminals to account. While the masterminds of the crime walk around freely, why has the government been unable to arrest them? Why were only his house and municipality spared? Everything else burns  why not his  once he becomes Prime Minister, everything will burn.
12. Incident Account of Former Member of Parliament Ram Narayan Bidari:

I am a legal professional and have been serving as a Senior Advocate for the past 43 years. On Bhadra 23, 2082, I had gone to a para-clinic in New Baneswhar for a blood test. I support the demands of the Gen Z movement but was not involved in it. After the blood test, I went directly to my private residence at Dhungead. Being aware of the events that had occurred throughout the day, I expressed my views through a Facebook status about the merciless killing of young students. I had written calling for respect for those who had become martyrs, compensation, formation of a judicial commission to investigate, and action against the guilty. Thereafter, exhausted after the day, I rested in my own room. On Bhadra 24, 2082, I watched the news early in the morning and was very worried. I discussed with my neighbors that the country was in trouble because the country's politics was not in a proper state. At approximately 10:30 AM, I ate food at home with all household members. Having decided to stay home since curfew had been imposed inside the Ring Road, while I was sitting in my room, voices came from the main gate of my house, and I saw some people had gathered below  they were chanting slogans. I went downstairs intending to have a dialogue with them, but family members stopped me from going among those people. The group of people continued chanting slogans nonstop. The content of the slogans was: 'KP, thief, leave the country; Home Minister, down with you; Prachanda's broker, down with you.' After that, I stayed inside the house without coming out. One person entered my house and, taking me by the hand, said 'No one will do anything to you, please go outside' and brought me out, while some other people headed toward rooms inside the house. The person who brought me out is a person with the surname Thapa who lives in Bal in the same locality. After I had come outside, other family members were also brought out and we were forced to go elsewhere for security reasons. Thereafter, my mobile phone, laptop, my wife's mobile phone, my son and daughter-in-law's laptop, my daughter's laptop, jewelry and cash in drawers, gold, silver, and other jewelry were looted and the house was also set on fire. My two-and-a-half-story house in my name and my wife Nanimaya Bidari's three-room house  both houses were set on fire and destroyed, leaving only ashes. Now I have no home anywhere in the country. In the compound of that house: 1 motorcycle, 1 electric scooter, 1 scooter with a government number plate, 1 Toyota Jeep in the name of former civil servant Rupchandra Bista, and 1 BYD car  totaling 5 vehicles  were all burned by those criminal individuals. This was not done by the Gen Z youth  the evidence for this can be clearly seen on social media including YouTube and TikTok. I have sent all those photos and videos by email and WhatsApp to the Thankot Police and to this Commission as well. I have also submitted a petition of this account to this Commission and to Thankot Police Circle, in which the faces of the criminals are clearly visible. I am deeply troubled by the fact that people came to my home in broad daylight to set it on fire, loot it, and stayed at the same location all day until the house was completely destroyed. Those criminals were confident that none of the state's agencies would arrive there while they happily chatted, laughed, and joked while setting a citizen's house on fire. This is the important matter that requires this Commission's attention. Property worth approximately NPR 50 million belonging to me was looted and burned  the details of which I have also mentioned in my petitions. I, an innocent citizen, have not worked in any branch of the state for four years and have never served in an executive position throughout my life. Through 42 years of legal practice, 50 years of hard work, my wife's profession, ancestral property, life insurance, Citizens Investment Trust  why and for what reasons was the property I and my family earned destroyed? This is a matter for state investigation. It is the state's duty and obligation to protect my property. If it could not do so, full compensation must be provided. I drafted the current constitution and the current government took oath and operates under this very constitution  this means the problem was not a mistake I made in drafting the constitution. I have only been critical of corrupt party leaders, corrupt individuals, fraudsters, and criminal leaders in the country. Was this crime committed against me because of these things I said? Otherwise I have no fault, nor does any member of my family. My sons and daughters have their own separate professions and businesses  why was such a great crime committed against them? Must the state not pay attention to this? May the inquiry commission's attention go to this matter. Destroying all three branches  the executive, judiciary, and legislature  along with the Head of State, and trying to destroy and kill even Nepal Police, is something that innocent young children (Gen Z) would never do. Patriots also do not engage in such activities. Therefore, it is the state's duty to keep the morale of security personnel high and to honor and compensate the police who became martyrs. No national servant would commit the merciless killing of innocent young children on Bhadra 23  may the Commission also pay attention to who did this and for what purpose. It is clear as daylight whose hand it was that some political parties operating in Nepal were not touched from top to bottom in any way, while other parties  including leaders and workers  were attacked, and the homes of ordinary citizens, politicians, or national servants who criticized like me were deliberately and selectively destroyed. Therefore, may the Commission's attention also go to this matter. Big leaders ruined the country and made the people miserable. The people were angry at them. Ordinary citizens like me were MPs four years ago and hold no position beyond that. I have always advocated for people-centered issues and opposed wrong things by those in power. From the age of 14 to this day, never having tolerated the wrong deeds of those in power, this has proved to be my misfortune today.
5.1.2. Statements and Incident Account Documents of Civil Servants:
13. Then Chief Secretary Ek Narayan Aryal:

At the informal meeting of Bhadra 22, 2082, conducting a security analysis of the demonstration called by the Gen Z generation for the following day, a plan was made to coordinate security arrangements so that no loss of lives or property would occur. On that same day while the demonstration was taking place, I was in my own office chamber at Singha Durbar. That night at around 10 PM, the 39th meeting of the National Security Council was held at Baluwatar under the chairmanship of the Prime Minister, where the heads of security agencies made oral presentations. The meeting received directives from the Prime Minister to coordinate security arrangements through the security agencies, and that political coordination would be done at the government level. We discussed the matter of effectively mobilizing the entire security apparatus through the Ministry of Home Affairs to minimize the impact of the events and ensure minimum loss of lives and property. At that meeting, no concrete intelligence information was presented indicating large-scale violence, arson, or major vandalism from social media platforms such as Discord and Reddit. Although I had personally heard that activity on social media had increased, a clear assessment that such a large-scale violent situation would arise was not received from the security agencies. Although it was estimated that a procession would go from Maitighar to Baneswhar, there had been no assessment that it would cause such enormous damage.

On the morning of Bhadra 24, at around 10 AM before the Prime Minister's resignation, the Home Secretary, Chief of Army Staff, and heads of all security agencies met at Baluwatar and informed him of the ground situation. After reports came that the situation was becoming increasingly serious following the Prime Minister's resignation and that the situation was beginning to go out of control, toward evening I, the Home Secretary, the Chief of Army Staff, the Inspector General of Police, the Inspector General of the Armed Police Force, and the Director General of the National Investigation Department collectively participated in a public appeal with the aim of making citizens feel the government's presence. The heads of security agencies also informally attempted to speak with some leaders of the agitators to convey the message that we can only exist if the nation exists. I, in coordination with the Chief of Army Staff, requested the Honorable President to quickly begin the government formation process. Prior to that, security arrangements had been made to prevent damage to sensitive agencies such as the Kathmandu District Police Office, foreign embassies, telecommunications, Nepal Electricity Authority, and electricity and water offices. Security arrangements were made for senior leaders and high-ranking individuals including serving ministers, and Pashupatinath Temple was saved from vandalism. Since the constitutional provision stipulates that the Honorable Prime Minister as chairperson of the Security Council must make the decision to impose a state of emergency and deploy the army, and since I was only an ex-officio member, I was not in a position to call a council meeting.

After the events of Bhadra 23 and 24, a virtual meeting was held on Bhadra 25 to coordinate the resumption of security agencies and government offices across the country including Singha Durbar. At that meeting, directives and information were provided regarding daily operations, security arrangements, lifeline services, and basic service management. I also directed the Home Secretary to hold a virtual meeting encompassing the Chief District Officers and District Security Chiefs of all 77 districts. On-site monitoring was also carried out at agencies that had been set on fire, including Singha Durbar, the Honorable Supreme Court, and Parliament House. Even in a situation where the formal presence of political parties and the government was weak, it was not easy to continue the airport operations, other basic services, and daily tasks  but all these were accomplished in coordination with the security agencies. Later, after Sushila Karki was appointed as Honorable Prime Minister and assumed office, work was also done on security meetings to manage peace and security, initial collection of damage data, and facilitation of the operations the government needed to undertake in the coming days.

The social media claims that I played a role in giving a letter from Nakkhu Prison and safely releasing Rabi Lamichhane, Chairperson of the Rastriya Swatantra Party, have no truth whatsoever. Such work does not fall within the responsibilities of the Chief Secretary  this is what I believe to be a conspiracy to personally defame me. The lack of coordination visible among security agencies cannot be blamed on any single institution alone. Even though the army should have been at the frontline after the curfew was imposed, the visible shortage of adequate equipment, barricades, and morale in the police is an indication of the weakness of overall security management. The state will not be strong and citizens will not feel security unless the security agencies are strong and motivated. My final conclusion is that administrative and policy reforms are indispensable to prevent such events from recurring. The need to keep civil servant and security personnel morale high, end political interference, ensure career development, provide adequate training, equipment, and social security guarantees, and emphasize the maintenance of good governance must be stressed. My understanding is that the events of Bhadra 23 and 24 occurred due to increasing public anger caused by factors such as weak service delivery, lack of public trust regarding corruption, politicization of the bureaucracy, and inadequate use of technology. Therefore, my firm conclusion is that if future governments adopt a transparent, accountable, and reform-oriented working style, only then will the foundation be laid to prevent such crises from recurring.
14. Pancha Prasad Pandey, Secretary-General of the Federal Parliament Secretariat:

I have been serving as Secretary of the House of Representatives since 2080 BS and as Secretary-General of the Federal Parliament since Magh of that same year. My main responsibilities as Secretary-General, in the capacity of administrative head of the secretariat, are to conduct structural and managerial work and to provide necessary directives, coordination, and monitoring to keep the proceedings of both houses and their committees running smoothly. Regarding security arrangements, I have not been given direct legal authority, but in my capacity as office chief, I have been playing a facilitative role through continuous coordination as needed with the Chief of Marshals Security, the Baneswhar Police Circle Chief, and the Inspector General of Police, along with relevant security agencies.

On Bhadra 23, since a National Assembly session had been called, I arrived at the Parliament building in New Baneswhar at 8 AM. On that day, assessing the then security situation due to the movement by the Gen Z generation, and after discussions with the National Assembly Chairperson and Secretary, a decision was made to postpone the session. In the afternoon, demonstrations outside the Parliament building became more aggressive, with stone-pelting and obstruction. The Parliament Secretariat was unable to go to Singha Durbar, and since there was no clear plan for what to do if demonstrators entered the Parliament building, I remained there until evening in an uncertain state where security personnel would stop them if they could and if they couldn't, whatever the protesters would do, they would do. It was only after the situation somewhat normalized at around 7:00 PM in the evening that I returned home.

On Bhadra 23, the situation around the Parliament building became progressively tense. Demonstrators broke through walls and barriers and entered inside, and security personnel immediately removed them. A shooting incident also occurred outside. Injured police and demonstrators received treatment inside the Parliament building from Dr. Pushpa Rijal, and some injured persons were sent to the hospital by ambulance. But after an ambulance was burned outside, the avenue for sending additional injured persons to the hospital was also virtually blocked. My feeling was that had the security personnel not tightened security arrangements outside, the Parliament building could have been burned on Bhadra 23 itself.

On Bhadra 24, since curfew had been imposed in Kathmandu and Lalitpur, I stayed home and was in continuous contact with Police Superintendent Bimal Basnet, Police Deputy Superintendent Hari Khatiwada, and secretariat staff. However, on that day, I felt that the security personnel appeared somewhat discouraged. I acknowledge the fact that there was no pre-planned Contingency Plan for minimizing damage to the Parliament building. The possibility that the Parliament building would fall into a 'high risk' category had not been spontaneously imagined beforehand, and since no serious Threat Label or advance warning came from security agencies, initiatives to strengthen infrastructure such as high walls, sturdy gates, boom barriers, and electric fences were also not undertaken. Additionally, the absence of formally organized structures such as ROE (Rules of Engagement) within the security agencies was also one aspect of the problem.

Although continuous coordination between security personnel and the Parliament Secretariat was occurring internally, the failure to identify the level of risk beforehand, the weakness visible in security on Bhadra 24, and the failure of access control within the parliamentary premises became the main causes of the events. The arson and vandalism of that day caused enormous damage to the Parliament Secretariat, parliamentary party offices, and the physical structures of the secretariat. 18 government and private vehicles were completely burned and destroyed, 7 sustained partial damage, 37 motorcycles/scooters were completely burned, furniture, office supplies, computers, audio-video equipment, ACs, CCTV cameras, and other materials were destroyed, and the parliamentary party offices of various parties were also damaged. However, the fact that there was no loss of human life  no deaths  in this incident can be taken as a positive aspect.

The primary responsibility for preventing the enormous damage caused by these days' demonstrations rests with the demonstrators, as the arson and vandalism were initiated by them. The second responsibility lies with the security personnel, who failed to adequately secure an extremely sensitive area. Third, the failure of civilian national servants to intervene in time by explaining the situation to the agitators is also visible as a weakness.

Since the Parliament building will be relocated inside the Singha Durbar premises in the future, my suggestion is that the government must integrate the parliament security system within the overall security plan of Singha Durbar itself. To enhance the level of security, a pre-estimation system, adequate equipment, strong infrastructure, clear Rules of Engagement, strengthening of inter-agency coordination, and regular risk assessment are essential. The events that occurred at the Parliament building have clearly shown the weaknesses of the security system, lack of preparedness, and deficiency in contingency planning. Although I played a coordinator and contact person role in the management of the incident, I accept with self-criticism that the level of risk could not be estimated beforehand. Although the physical damage was extremely extensive, the fact that there was no loss of human life can be taken as a positive aspect.
14. Then Home Secretary Gokarna Mani Duwadi:

Before the events, on the evening of Bhadra 22, the Central Security Committee meeting was held at the Ministry of Home Affairs in Singha Durbar. At that meeting, a briefing was received from the heads of security agencies and Chief District Officers that the younger generation had organized a demonstration and the organizers had stated it would be peaceful. On that occasion, although the ministry's policy was to prevent the demonstration from becoming provocative and to exercise restraint, no written decision had been made. I was not aware at that time about the digital platforms such as Reddit and Discord being used by the Gen Z youth generation. I have clarified that the management of situations that could arise from closing social media did not fall within the Home Ministry's area of operation, and that I had no information on that matter.

On the morning of Bhadra 23, I was present at a State Affairs and Good Governance Committee meeting of the House of Representatives at Singha Durbar. Reporting on movement and other activities was normally done by noting in the ministry's control, and in some situations, information also came from Chief District Officers and heads of security agencies. On that same day, at around 11:30-12:00 noon, after receiving information from the Director General of the National Investigation Department that demonstrators were breaking through the security cordon and entering the Parliament building, I left the meeting and reached the ministry. Since Section 6(a) of the Local Administration Act, 2028 grants the Chief District Officer the authority to impose curfew, I was informed by phone that the then Chief District Officer of Kathmandu had imposed curfew, and during the implementation of the curfew, directives were given to the security agencies to work in a restrained and legal manner. After the curfew order, since the Chief District Officer of Kathmandu requested the Nepal Army's assistance and sought coordination, I immediately called the Chief of Army Staff twice to request the necessary assistance and coordination. I received oral reports from the heads of security agencies that large numbers of demonstrators and security personnel had been injured during the demonstrations, and all those details were immediately communicated to the Home Minister.

In the situation where many casualties had occurred in the tragic events at the Parliament building premises, my attention and that of all security agency heads was focused on treatment and facilitation of the injured. On that day, reports were received of demonstrations across the country and incidents occurring in many places. At the security briefing held at the Prime Minister's residence at Baluwatar in the evening, information was received that the situation in the Parliament building area had come under control. I learned that tragic events had occurred during the demonstrations, but I did not have detailed information at that time about exactly who had been shot.

On the evening of Bhadra 23, a review of the events was conducted at Baluwatar. In that process, discussions were held on the Nepal Army and all security agencies effectively coordinating to address potential security challenges and make appropriate security arrangements for Vital Installations. As the Home Minister resigned from his position while the situation had not yet come under full control  in the difficult circumstances arising from the human casualties in the Bhadra 23 Gen Z movement  I began reporting security information to the Chief Secretary.

On Bhadra 24, 2082, at around 8 AM, I reached the Ministry of Home Affairs at Singha Durbar and then the Prime Minister's residence at Baluwatar. On that day, after receiving information from the heads of security agencies about arson, vandalism, and looting at private residences of party leaders, party offices, police offices, government offices, and commercial establishments in various parts of the country including the Kathmandu Valley, I reported to the Chief Secretary. The Chief Secretary said he would inform the then Prime Minister, but no concrete directives could be received on that matter.

Since the jurisdiction to issue, with a letter, the release of Rabi Lamichhane, Chairperson of the Rastriya Swatantra Party, who was in custody at Nakkhu Prison in Lalitpur, for security reasons does not rest with the Home Secretary, I have not given any order or directive to that effect. Similarly, on Bhadra 24, the orders to prepare a list of detainees from the communication sets of the Nepal Police Headquarters or the Valley Police Office, to release detainees if such a situation arose, to keep their weapons, communication sets, and vehicles safe, and to not use any force other than tear gas  I did not give such orders, and I had no knowledge of such matters. After the Prime Minister's resignation in the afternoon of Bhadra 24, since it appeared necessary for the heads of all security agencies to sit together in one place and coordinate in the difficult situation in the country for managing peace and security, we all gathered at the Army Headquarters in Bhadrakali and focused on coordination. In the same process, a public call was also made to resolve the problem through negotiation and dialogue.

Regarding the provision of relief and compensation to persons who died and were injured during the demonstrations of Bhadra 23 and 24, while I was still in office, a decision was made by the Council of Ministers on a proposal from the Ministry of Home Affairs. According to that decision, provisions were made to declare those who died during the demonstrations as martyrs, to provide NPR 1.5 million in relief to each deceased person's family, for the Ministry of Health to arrange free treatment for the injured, and for the Ministry of Urban Development to construct a memorial at an appropriate location. On those days, the Ministry of Home Affairs effectively coordinated and facilitated the management of the bodies of the deceased and the treatment of the injured. On the afternoon of that same day, while I was engaged in managing peace and security, I later learned that an unknown group had entered my private residence at Ward No. 2, Nagarjun Municipality, Kathmandu District, and vandalized, set fire to, and looted it.

Although the main causes of the events of Bhadra 23 and 24 included the government's control of social media and the demand to control corruption and maintain good governance, my view is that this must be seen in connection with the country's overall governance system, economic and social conditions, and the effectiveness of service delivery. To prevent such events from recurring in the future, I have emphasized: making public service delivery technology-friendly, quick, effective, and quality-oriented; controlling delays, hassles, and middlemen visible in service delivery; ensuring political appointments are based on merit, competence, and capability; creating an environment for employment and investment; making government performance transparent and accountable; completing development and construction works on time; and ensuring equal access to state resources and equitable distribution for all.

It appears necessary to equip agencies under the Ministry of Home Affairs with physical infrastructure, system development, and training to make them efficient and citizen-friendly. Since the District Administration Office is the leading and coordinating agency of the entire district, it must be developed as a model office. My understanding is that since the Chief District Officer must also perform quasi-judicial work and peace and security matters are serious and sensitive, personnel management as a Ministerial Cadre at the Ministry of Home Affairs and subordinate agencies is necessary. I have also pointed to the need for legal reforms to simplify service delivery, develop and use technology, and provide additional responsibility and authority while facilitating coordination among federal, provincial, and local levels.

Since security teams deployed in the field use force based on their analysis of the situation and circumstances, issuing direct orders such as 'Cease Fire' from the Home Secretary does not fall within my legal jurisdiction. I accept that since information was received that the Gen Z movement would be peaceful, it was not possible to anticipate that such a large-scale violence, arson, and vandalism would occur. Although inter-agency coordination mechanisms such as the Central Security Committee and District Security Committee exist among security agencies and have been in operation, my view is that how effective those mechanisms were in the events of Bhadra 23 and 24 is a matter for the Commission to analyze comprehensively. Technical aspects such as Parliament building perimeter, buffer zone, escape routes, safety corridors, layered defense, and zeroing naturally fall within the operational areas of security agencies deployed in the field and the District Security Committee, so it is natural that I would not have direct knowledge of those matters. Additionally, the criteria for distinguishing between demonstrators and bystander citizens, crowd control SOPs, and special training matters also fall within the technical responsibilities of the security agencies.

Overall, I interpret my role as that of a coordinating body that operates within legal boundaries. Since the use of force, deployment, and technical decisions are directed by the field and the law, I assert that I did not give any orders exceeding my authority, and I express my confidence that the overall truth and responsibility determination of the incident will emerge from the Commission's impartial analysis.
16. Mrs. Radhika Aryal, Secretary of the Ministry of Communication and Information Technology:

I have been working in the civil service for 21 years and have been serving as Secretary of the Ministry of Communication and Information Technology for the past 17 months. I appeared before the Inquiry Commission and presented my views regarding the demonstrations by the Gen Z generation on Bhadra 23 and 24, 2082, and the decisions related to social media regulation in connection therewith. My statement is focused on clarifying the background of the regulatory decision, its legal basis, the implementation process, and the effects arising from it.

In my understanding, the root causes of those events were demands for raising voices against corruption, demanding good governance, dissatisfaction against Nepo-Kids/Nepo-Babies, and demands related to the smooth operation of social media. I experienced that the movement, which had initially been declared peaceful, suddenly turned violent, and the circumstances created by the tragic events of the first day made the situation of the second day even more complex.

I wish to clarify regarding the general understanding that a 'ban' was imposed on social media  no direct ban was imposed by the ministry. Only a regulatory decision was made, in accordance with the Directive on Regulating the Use of Social Media, 2080, and the Supreme Court's order, to compel social media platforms to register in Nepal and establish an official contact point. The provision was made to temporarily deactivate platforms that did not register within the stipulated 7 days, and to reactivate them the moment they registered. The legal basis for this decision was the Supreme Court's contempt of court order and the Council of Ministers' decision dated 2082/05/09.

Regarding Risk Assessment, since social media is directly connected to information, education, health, business, and employment in Nepal, discussions were held among the ministry, Nepal Telecommunications Authority, and other relevant agencies about the economic, social, and diplomatic impacts that could arise from it. However, no written security risk assessment or suggestion was received from the police, Ministry of Home Affairs, or other security agencies.

Public notices were repeatedly issued through national daily newspapers, the ministry's website, and other media to inform the youth and general public. Social media was not deactivated on the grounds that it was a medium for escalating violence: the attempt at regulation was made with the objective of controlling misuse through misinformation, cybercrime, and fake identity.

The reason why alternative measures such as Specific Content Removal, Flagging, or Algorithm-based Regulation could not be immediately implemented was precisely the absence of formal presence and responsible contact points of the concerned social media platforms in Nepal. My view is that only after registration and establishment of official contact points could such measures be effectively implemented.

I reject the allegation that freedom of expression under Article 17 of the Constitution was violated. Our objective was not to control expression, but to bring social media within the legal framework and ensure responsible and accurate information flow. The National Human Rights Commission was not given prior notice. The deactivation period was limited  only about four days. All social media was not completely shut down: since platforms like Viber and TikTok continued to operate, there was no direct disruption to citizen services.

For the future, I wish to point out the need to establish a clear legal basis for social media regulation, strengthen diplomatic dialogue with multinational platforms, encourage the development of domestic social media, and quickly pass the relevant bill registered in the Federal Parliament with necessary revisions. Overall, my clear position is that the action taken regarding social media was not a 'ban' but an attempt at regulation and management.
17. Joint Secretary Ananda Kafle, Head of the Security and Coordination Division, Ministry of Home Affairs:

I have been managing the responsibilities of three branches under the Security and Coordination Division  the Peace, Security and Crime Control Branch; the Local Administration Branch; and the Border and Immigration Administration Branch. In addition, I have also been serving as the spokesperson for the Ministry of Home Affairs. In accordance with my area of work, I have been organizing security-related meetings and coordinating with security agencies, inquiring about complaints received from anywhere regarding crime-related matters, and making necessary coordination regarding security agencies related to my division as directed by the Honorable Minister and Secretary. I myself do not bear the responsibility of giving direct orders to the security force. I have been informing the Honorable Minister and Secretary about information received through Chief District Officers and coordinating as necessary.
